nativeRange.habitat: Sandy areas near mangroves, seagrass beds, and coral or rubble. The species is benthic and reef-associated, typically found at depths of 0 to 20 m.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on small fish and crustaceans, and has been documented consuming octopuses in the wild. In captivity, tends to accept chopped marine meats (shrimp, squid, silversides); will generally consume most invertebrates and small fish within reach.

goodWith: fish too large to be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: Dan Schofield; 2048x1536. Sharp side-profile of a live, vividly coloured individual showing the diagnostic bright-blue rings and rosettes and raised turret eyes on tan body; whole fish in frame.
Andra Waagmeester; 2048x1155. Complete top-down view over sand, sharp, showing full blue-and-yellow ringed pattern; good complementary angle to the profile shot.
Waielbi; 3400x1700. High-res close-up of the blue-ringed camouflage pattern on a live individual; head edge slightly cropped so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: The Assorted Flounder (Bothus lunatus) is a reef-associated flatfish that lies flat on the aquarium floor and partially buries itself in sand to ambush prey. It tends to consume any small fish or ornamental invertebrate within reach, making tankmate selection an important consideration for intermediate saltwater keepers.
careNotes: The Assorted Flounder, also sold as the Peacock Flounder or Plate Fish, is a laterally compressed flatfish that rests on its side on the aquarium floor, with both eyes migrating to the left side of the body during development. A fine sandy substrate is generally considered essential for this species to engage in natural camouflage behavior and to reduce stress. It has been observed partially burying itself in sand while ambushing small fish and crustaceans. Some retailers categorize it as easy to keep, but its predatory nature and large adult size — up to around 46 cm based on scientific records — suggest that intermediate is a more realistic assessment for long-term care. The Assorted Flounder trade name covers specimens from Western Atlantic populations (Florida, Bermuda, the Bahamas, and Brazil) and occasionally Eastern Atlantic variants from Ascension Island and the Gulf of Guinea. While not a coral predator, it tends to readily consume small ornamental invertebrates and any fish small enough to fit in its mouth, so tank selection should account for this.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperature range, native range, taxonomy, habitat, and diet. FishBase max size (46 cm TL) is larger than LiveAquaria's 14 in (35.6 cm); temperature range differs slightly (FishBase 25.2–28°C vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C). Values averaged per playbook rules.
